 {"id":"wa-zehp","title":"Implement read-only endpoints: /panes /events /search (shared query layer)","description":"# Task: Implement read-only web endpoints

## Goal
Expose minimal read-only endpoints for dashboards, while preserving wa’s safety and schema-stability guarantees.

## Endpoints (v0)
- `GET /health`
- `GET /panes`
- `GET /events?unhandled=1&pane_id=...&limit=...`
- `GET /search?q=...&pane_id=...&since=...&limit=...`

## Design constraints
- Reuse the same query/model code as CLI/robot.
  - The web server should not reimplement data access.
- Responses are JSON with stable schemas and stable ordering.
- Apply the same redaction pipeline as robot/audit/export.

## Pagination & limits
- All list endpoints must be bounded.
  - enforce default and max `limit`
  - provide stable ordering (e.g., newest-first for events)

## Error handling
- Return structured JSON errors with stable error codes.
- Never leak internal DB paths or sensitive config in errors.

## Testing
- Covered by the web server tests bead (`wa-nu4.3.6.4`):
  - schema parseability
  - redaction
  - bind defaults

## Acceptance Criteria
- Endpoints return data when watcher is running.
- Responses remain schema-stable and redacted.
- List endpoints enforce sane defaults and max limits.

 {"id":"wa-zeyh","title":"Coordination primitives: multi-pane selection, group locks, safe broadcast semantics","description":"# Task: Coordination primitives

## Goal
Define the primitives required for safe multi-pane workflows ("swarm" behavior) without turning wa into a chaos machine.

Multi-pane automation is powerful but risky: we need deterministic selection, locking, and per-pane auditing.

## Concepts
### Pane groups
- Define grouping strategies:
  - by domain
  - by inferred agent
  - by project (cwd-based)
- Group selection must be deterministic and explainable.

### Group locks
- Build on per-pane workflow locks to support multi-pane coordination:
  - lock acquisition across N panes is all-or-nothing (or explicitly partial with clear reporting)
  - timeouts and deadlock avoidance

### Safe broadcast semantics
- Observe each pane first.
- Only act on panes that pass preconditions (prompt state, no gap, not alt-screen, etc.).
- Execute actions with policy gating + rate limiting.
- Record per-pane results:
  - allowed/denied
  - verification success/failure
  - timing

## Testing strategy
- Unit tests with synthetic panes:
  - group selection and determinism
  - lock acquisition behavior
  - partial failure reporting

## Acceptance Criteria
- A workflow can target N panes and produce an auditable per-pane outcome.
- Preconditions prevent “spray and pray” broadcasting.

 {"id":"wa-zrgi","title":"Performance benchmarks and budgets: Criterion suite with regression guards","description":"# Performance benchmarks and budgets (Criterion)

## Goal
Establish benchmarks for all performance-critical paths and prevent regressions with explicit, documented budgets.

This system sits on the hot path of “every command the user runs” (watch loop, pattern matching, policy checks). Performance regressions are user-visible.

## Benchmark suite (Criterion)
Benchmarks must target *real* hot paths and be runnable locally:

```rust
// benches/pattern_engine.rs
criterion_group!(
    pattern_benchmarks,
    bench_bloom_check,
    bench_aho_corasick_match,
    bench_full_pattern_pipeline,
);

// benches/ingest.rs
criterion_group!(
    ingest_benchmarks,
    bench_delta_extraction,
    bench_segment_persistence,
    bench_fts_indexing,
);
```

## What we benchmark (minimum set)
- Quick reject / prefilter:
  - `memchr`/bloom/no-match fast paths
  - worst-case “near match but reject” path
- Pattern pipeline:
  - no-hit text (must be very fast)
  - one-hit typical text
  - extraction path with captures
- Ingest/deltas:
  - delta extraction on typical scrollback
  - gap detection path
- Storage:
  - write queue throughput (bounded queue)
  - FTS indexing cost for typical segment sizes
- Query surfaces:
  - typical FTS query latency (scoped)
  - worst-case query guardrails (bounded)

## Performance budgets (absolute ceilings)
Budgets are set as “this must feel snappy to users” ceilings. Exact numbers may be tuned after first implementation, but budgets must exist before optimization work is called done.

| Operation | Budget (target ceiling) | Notes |
|-----------|--------------------------|-------|
| Bloom filter check | < 1µs | hot path prefilter |
| Pattern match (no hit) | < 10µs | must be cheap for most text |
| Pattern match (with hit) | < 100µs | includes extraction |
| Delta extraction | < 1ms | per pane per tick typical |
| FTS insert (single) | < 5ms | typical segment size |
| FTS query (typical) | < 50ms | scoped query; “feels instant” |
| Startup (no patterns) | < 100ms | CLI responsiveness |

## Regression detection strategy (CI realities)
Perf measurement is noisy on shared runners. The plan must be robust:

- Local dev loop:
  - `cargo bench` is the source of truth for tuning.
  - Provide a short “budget check” summary line per benchmark so developers can self-serve.

- CI loop (two-tier):
  1) **PR job (warn-only by default)**
     - Run benches and upload `criterion` output + a short summary artifact.
     - Fail only on catastrophic regressions (e.g., >2x slower OR exceeding a hard ceiling by a large margin).
  2) **Main/perf job (enforce budgets)**
     - Prefer a stable environment (self-hosted runner if available).
     - Enforce budgets strictly and record historical trend artifacts.

- Baselines:
  - Store a baseline artifact from main (or a tagged commit) and compare PR results against it.
  - Comparisons must ignore obviously-noisy fields and focus on timing deltas.

## Reporting
Example (desired UX):
```
$ cargo bench --bench pattern_engine

bloom_check         time:   [0.8 µs 0.9 µs 1.0 µs]  ✓ (budget: 1µs)
aho_corasick_match  time:   [8.2 µs 8.5 µs 8.8 µs]  ✓ (budget: 10µs)
full_pipeline       time:   [45 µs 48 µs 51 µs]     ✓ (budget: 100µs)
```

On regression, the report must include:
- which benchmark regressed
- old vs new timings
- which budget was exceeded (if any)
- where to find the artifacts (CI URL/path)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Criterion benchmarks exist for the minimum hot paths listed above.
- [ ] Budgets are documented and checked by an automated “budget check” step.
- [ ] CI produces artifacts for every run (raw criterion + summarized results).
- [ ] CI regression detection works (warn-only on PR; enforced on stable perf job).

## Testing
- Benchmark harness self-checks:
  - benches compile and run on supported platforms.
  - the budget-checking logic is covered by unit tests (parsing/threshold evaluation).
- CI regression guard tests:
  - include a small synthetic “known regression” fixture in test mode to verify:
    - the budget checker fails when it should
    - output includes the expected summary fields","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-18T17:54:35.705060335Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-02-11T00:47:48.287928-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-08T08:39:30.156476833Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-zrgi","depends_on_id":"wa-vpud","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T00:22:31Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"wa-zssw","title":"Connection pooling for WezTerm: reuse mux connections when available","description":"
# Connection Pooling for WezTerm

## Purpose
Reduce overhead of WezTerm CLI calls by pooling connections to the mux server.

## Current Behavior
Each WezTerm CLI call spawns a new process, connects to mux, executes, and disconnects.

## Proposed Behavior
When using vendored mode (direct mux protocol):
```rust
pub struct WezTermPool {
    connections: Vec<MuxConnection>,
    max_size: usize,
    idle_timeout: Duration,
}

impl WezTermPool {
    pub async fn execute<T>(&self, cmd: MuxCommand) -> Result<T> {
        let conn = self.acquire().await?;
        let result = conn.execute(cmd).await;
        self.release(conn);
        result
    }
}
```

## Benefits
- Avoid process spawn overhead (~10ms per call)
- Reduce socket churn
- Enable connection keepalive
- Batch multiple operations efficiently

## Prerequisites
- Requires vendored mode (bd-nu4.4.1)
- Direct mux protocol access

## Fallback
CLI mode: No pooling possible, but can batch commands.

## Benchmark Targets
- With pooling: ~1ms per operation
- Without pooling: ~10-20ms per operation
- 10x improvement for high-frequency operations

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Connection pool implemented for vendored mode
- [ ] Idle connection cleanup
- [ ] Graceful fallback for CLI mode
- [ ] Benchmark proves improvement

## Testing
- Criterion benchmarks with budgets and baseline comparison.
- Microbenchmarks for the specific optimization.
- CI regression guard with perf logs.

 {"id":"wa-zv77","title":"E2E script: dry-run mode (wa send/workflow --dry-run previews)","description":"# E2E script: dry-run mode (previews for wa send / wa workflow / robot)

## Goal
Validate end-to-end that `--dry-run` produces accurate previews **without mutating anything**:
- correct target resolution (pane/domain/cwd/agent)
- correct policy evaluation (allow/deny/require-approval)
- correct “what would happen” step plan for workflows
- deterministic, schema-valid JSON output for machines

This is the integration proof for the dry-run epic (`wa-1pe`) using the shared E2E harness.

## Why this matters
Dry-run is the primary trust-building UX feature:
- users can explore safely
- robots can plan before acting
- reduces mistakes and support burden

## Key constraints
- **Zero side effects**:
  - must not send input to panes
  - must not acquire workflow locks
  - must not mark events handled
  - must not create workflow executions
  - may optionally write an audit entry tagged as a *dry-run evaluation* (explicitly non-action)
- **Deterministic**: no fixed sleeps; use bounded waits/quiescence.
- **Machine-stable**: tests should prefer `--format json` outputs (avoid scraping rich/TTY output).
- Uses the standard harness contract (`wa-4vx.10.6`) and runner (`wa-4vx.10.11`).

## Test setup
- Start a WezTerm mux server and spawn dummy panes that:
  - can echo any received input to prove whether a send occurred
  - can deterministically emit patterns to create events for workflow previews
- Run `wa watch` in an isolated E2E workspace.

## Scenarios

### 1) `wa send --dry-run` preview quality
- Run: `wa send --pane 3 --dry-run "hello" --format json`
- Assert preview includes:
  - resolved pane info (pane_id, title, cwd, agent)
  - policy decision + reasons (capabilities, safety gate)
  - an explicit “would send” description (bytes/line endings/control codes)
  - an explicit “would wait-for” plan if applicable

### 2) `wa send --dry-run` on a denied state
- Create a deterministic unsafe state (fixture / dummy alt-screen).
- Run: `wa send --pane 3 --dry-run "hello" --format json`
- Assert:
  - decision is deny/require-approval as expected
  - the preview explains remediation (e.g., exit AltScreen)
  - **no text was sent** (dummy pane echo confirms)

### 3) Preview vs actual: stable-field match
Goal: the preview’s *decision* and *target* must match the actual path.
- Capture preview JSON: `wa send … --dry-run --format json > artifacts/send_preview.json`
- Execute actual send: `wa send … --format json > artifacts/send_actual.json`
- Compare only stable fields:
  - `target.pane_id`
  - `policy.decision` + `policy.rule_id` (if present)
  - `action_kind`
  - (ignore timestamps, durations, correlation ids)

### 4) `wa workflow run --dry-run` step plan preview
- Ensure a deterministic unhandled event exists (dummy pane emits marker).
- Run: `wa workflow run handle_compaction --pane 3 --dry-run --format json`
- Assert preview includes:
  - step list in order (with step ids/names)
  - per-step policy checks (or a summarized policy gate)
  - expected waits/verifications
  - explicit note that no locks will be acquired and no DB state will change

### 5) Robot dry-run output (schema + no mutation)
- Run: `wa robot send --pane 3 --dry-run "hello" --format json`
- Run: `wa robot workflow handle_compaction 3 --dry-run --format json` (or the canonical robot workflow invocation)
- Assert outputs validate against the dry-run JSON schemas and include:
  - `decision` / `would_execute=false`
  - `steps_preview` (for workflows)

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`) with prerequisites and default inclusion status.

## Artifacts
- `send_preview.json`
- `send_actual.json`
- `workflow_preview.json`
- `dry_run_vs_actual.json` (comparison result)
- `wa_watch.log`
- `events.jsonl`
- `audit_slice.jsonl` (to prove “no action” vs “action”)

## Logging contract
Log per scenario:
- command invoked (redacted)
- timeout settings
- comparison summary (stable fields)

Example:
```
[DRYRUN_E2E] scenario=send_preview pane=3
[DRYRUN_E2E] decision=allow would_execute=false
[DRYRUN_E2E] scenario=preview_vs_actual stable_match=true
```

## Testing
- Determinism:
  - no fixed sleeps
  - every wait has a timeout and emits actionable failure output
- No-mutation proofs (pick at least two independent signals):
  - dummy pane did not echo received input
  - no new `send_text` audit action exists (only optional `dry_run` audit)
  - no `workflow_executions` row was created on dry-run
- Schema:
  - JSON outputs validate against the documented dry-run schema(s)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Dry-run output is informative (target + policy + plan).
- [ ] Dry-run never mutates panes/locks/handled state.
- [ ] Preview decisions match actual decisions on stable fields.
- [ ] Robot dry-run output is schema-valid and deterministic.
- [ ] Artifacts/logging make failures easy to debug.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-18T19:11:33.257051753Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-02-11T00:47:48.222149-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-06T03:32:32.007423661Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-zv77","depends_on_id":"wa-2vr1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T00:22:31Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-zv77","depends_on_id":"wa-mxzp","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T00:22:31Z","created_by":"import"}]}
